content:
Let me add my two cents to this discussion... Having commercially worked with Flutter for around two years now, the most important thing that I learnt was that **Flutter is a UI toolkit**. There wouldn't be anything surprising in this statement - it even says that on the main Flutter website - if people didn't forget about this.

Flutter is a UI toolkit, and what it does well is UI. If you're trying to create a CPU-heavy application that relies on expensive data processing or uses complicated algorithms and consumes larga amounts of data, then you should NOT use a UI toolkit for this purpose. Business Logic should not be mixed with the UI.

Of course, with Dart we can do much more than just UI, but let's not forget the main purpose fo this toolkit.

I've tried a number of approaches, including using headless Flutter - which proved to be a total disaster. Not because it doesn't work, but because we have problems explaining to other developers how it works. The maintenance burdon it adds up is just not worth it. Not too mention testing such a solution. The last time I tried, Flutter Driver wouldn't handle it at all.

If your application really does need the processing power, IMHO, you have three options:
- move your logic to the server;
- move your logic to the native platform - you can use Kotlin Native for this - and use Event/Method channels to only provide the View Models to your UI (I think that's what OLX did: https://tech.olx.com/fast-prototypes-with-flutter-kotlin-native-d7ce5cfeb5f1);
- don't use Flutter and look for another framework (Xamarin/Blazor might be a good fit for this as C# offers a more sophisticated multithreading environment than Dart).

All of the above have their shortcomings, but trying to stretch a library/framework to do what it wasn't really designed for isn't ideal either.
Had not the [isolate_handler](https://pub.dev/packages/isolate_handler) solved this problem？

> > Create a new plugin registry etc. for each isolate BUT within the same FlutterEngine,
> 
> This could be a good option, but only if the engine always registers plugins by itself.
> 
> But this only happens if all plugins in the GeneratedPluginRegistrant.
> https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/f7d241fd8a889fadf8ab3f9d57918be3d986b4be/shell/platform/android/io/flutter/embedding/engine/FlutterEngine.java#L330-L344
> 
> Creating additional callbacks is not an obvious behavior.

Another improvement to this second idea is to make have plugins loaded lazily in the new isolate. Normally, if a project depends on many plugins, the `GeneratedPluginRegistrant` will be quite large and depending on how efficient a plugin's initialisation routine is, this may contribute to some of the overheads. Exactly how to implement this is another story. Platform channels aren't owned by plugins, so currently sending a message over a channel can't be used to trigger the platform side of a plugin to be instantiated. So either method channels would need to be associated with plugins, or there would need to be an additional mechanism that the Dart side of a plugin could call on to initialise itself lazily before the first method channel is created, and that mechanism could trigger the instantiation of the platform side of that plugin within the isolate.

```

import 'package:battery/battery.dart';
import 'package:flutter/foundation.dart';
import 'package:flutter/widgets.dart';

Future<void> main() async {
  await printBatteryLevel();
  await compute(printBatteryLevel, null);
}

Future<void> printBatteryLevel([dynamic message]) async {
  WidgetsFlutterBinding.ensureInitialized();
  print(await Battery().batteryLevel);
}
```

@TahaTesser 
please see this [comment](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/16846#issuecomment-383752606) 

> Platform messages are only supported from the main isolate. [...]

This behavior is **_expected_** and has for the label `severe: new feature`
this issue should be considered as **_feature request_** or _**proposal**_ and not as **_bug_**

you are right mine is not a good example, please ignore it

If the isolate is spawned via a new FlutterEngine on the host platform this will work.  The next version of Flutter will have a new API available that is called FlutterEngineGroup which allows one to create FlutterEngines at a fraction of the cost they used to be (~180Kb).  The host platform can then be used as a dispatcher of messages between multiple isolates.  We do something similar here with add-to-app: https://github.com/flutter/samples/tree/master/add_to_app/multiple_flutters   The same approach would work for a plugin I believe.  I haven't tried setting the entry point for a spawned flutter engine to a function in a plugin, yet.

Thanks @gaaclarke - this is basically what the flutter_isolate plugin does at the moment (just with the "old" approach of instantiating a new FlutterEngine), however a more lightweight approach would definitely be welcome. It looks like the "new" FlutterEngineGroup API isn't yet available, you mentioned "the next version", does that mean there's a targeted version for release, or just "it's in the pipeline"?

Also in case others are interested, I've created a repository to track progress reimplementing the flutter_isolate plugin functionality to take advantage of this new API:

https://github.com/nmfisher/flutter_isolate_engine_group

Obviously it doesn't offer any benefit at the moment, but I can come back to it when the new API is released.

> Also in case others are interested, I've created a repository to track progress reimplementing the flutter_isolate plugin functionality to take advantage of this new API:

Nice work! I have been maintaining flutter_isolate for the past year or so since I needed it as a dependency for the audio_service plugin. The original author is very open to assigning rights to someone else who is interested in taking over the project, so you should definitely consider contacting him. If I had the time, I would probably take flutter_isolate in the same direction to take advantage of this new API but we probably don't need to duplicate the effort here so it may be something worth thinking about.

And, just another support, with [isolate_handler](https://pub.dev/packages/isolate_handler) we actively rely on the functionality provided by flutter_isolate, watching closely any progress in this area. :-)

@deakjahn I was also reminded of your package when I wrote the previous comment, and perhaps you might also be interested in getting access to the flutter_isolate repository. I have just posted [this issue](https://github.com/rmawatson/flutter_isolate/issues/70) on flutter_isolate repo to see if we can find someone interested in continuing with the flutter_isolate name.

@nmfisher @ryanheise

> Nice work! I have been maintaining flutter_isolate for the past year or so since I needed it as a dependency for the audio_service plugin. The original author is very open to assigning rights to someone else who is interested in taking over the project, so you should definitely consider contacting him

More than happy anyone who is willing, to take over the flutter_isolate repo. Although I think @ryanheise has full control to add anyone to this repo too.
Definitely worth considering to make things less disjointed and confusing or any existing users.

> It looks like the "new" FlutterEngineGroup API isn't yet available, you mentioned "the next version", does that mean there's a targeted version for release, or just "it's in the pipeline"?

FlutterEngineGroup has been released in Flutter 2.0.  Sample code can be found here: https://github.com/flutter/samples/tree/master/add_to_app/multiple_flutters

Sorry @gaaclarke, that was lazy phrasing on my part - I meant to ask when it will be stable (since it's marked as "experimental" at https://flutter.dev/docs/development/add-to-app/multiple-flutters and [the source](https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/master/shell/platform/android/io/flutter/embedding/engine/FlutterEngineGroup.java) says "Do not ship production code with this".

> Sorry @gaaclarke, that was lazy phrasing on my part - I meant to ask when it will be stable (since it's marked as "experimental" at https://flutter.dev/docs/development/add-to-app/multiple-flutters and [the source](https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/master/shell/platform/android/io/flutter/embedding/engine/FlutterEngineGroup.java) says "Do not ship production code with this".

Sure, it doesn't say "Do not ship production code with this."  It tries to communicate the risk of using it.  The reason is that it is a very complicated feature that depends on an experimental Dart feature.  We have tested it locally and have had a few customers ship products using it already.  Those customers have in some cases had to work with us to fix bugs they found.  Also, it's a warning about the API changing, which should be a slight nuance at worst.

If someone wants to use the feature in production they should first test the feature mimicking their use-case.  Then they should be willing to work with our team to fix any issues they run into.  Depending on the resources / skill of your team that might not be feasible honestly.

So basically, just make sure you do the correct calculus that factors in the risk / reward before using it.  That will be the case until more customers ship with it.  Until then we've decided to error on the side of caution.  If no one uses it, then a year later we say, "Well, no one has had any issues.  Let's remove the warning," then people start using it; that defeats the purpose of the warning obviously.  I hope that makes it clear.

edit: The java docstring did say "Do not ship production code with this," removing it in https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/25544
Thanks for taking the time to explain @gaaclarke, that's clearer now.

> And, just another support, with [isolate_handler](https://pub.dev/packages/isolate_handler) we actively rely on the functionality provided by flutter_isolate, watching closely any progress in this area. :-)

For now, the `isolate_handler` is a good solution:

https://pub.dev/packages/isolate_handler

All the solutions above seem not to have support  for Desktop.

Any ideas where to go from here?

There is a design doc out for [FFI Platform Channels](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1QhCs6RnDHG9ltYDOx9jaPg-s4sxOTFMC_kt3AZdf25g/edit?usp=sharing), they wouldn't have the same limitation about which isolate can call them. They, however, are a bit lower-level and require that you handle your own thread-safety and respond synchronously.

Hi all,
We are using [ffmpeg_kit_flutter](https://pub.dev/packages/ffmpeg_kit_flutter) to encode large videos in mobile clients before uploading them to the server. 
Being able to do this in an isolate will largely increase UI performance of the app. the isolate will let us know when the encoding is finished and will return the saved file path.
However because of the root cause of this issue, this can not work now.
Do you have any suggestions? I thought the new flutter isolate mechanisms allowing to create flutter engines with a fraction of the cost would have helped. Is it something else?

I don't know why you @ replied me. You didn't respond to my talking points. I honestly cannot follow your points. It seems we can agree that plugins are important. As a senior super duper elite coder I need something more than isolates. And I would appreciate if my isolate didn't crash when I call a plugin. I've already moved on and solved this issue with many elite coder hacking skills. I'm hoping dart will add more advanced threads in the future. Cheers.

@j3g Simply because I mentioned a solution to your problem. There are two plugins currently that make isolates able to call plugins. Without crashes. It's up to you to use them and have a perfect, functioning solution, or not.

I simply tried to explain why it isn't probably necessary to have this functionality in the core isolates and why it's enough to have plugins fill the gap.

@deakjahn one (of the many) but a very strong one, for which it could be useful to call platform channel methods in another isolate is image compression.

I may want to use the native code to have the fastest possible image compression, and I would like to do that in an isolate.

With so many likes and people requesting this my humble humble humble opinione is that P5 does not reflect well the issue and the demand.

Best regards

@mariopepe I agree there are valid reasons for using platform channels in a non-main isolate, but I would suggest that for the use case of image compression, a much better solution would be to use Dart FFI, which does work well within any Isolate.
I think isolate and background thread  are different instances one for dart and one for native.
@deakjahn Can we use the packages you have suggested for running [ffmpeg_kit](https://github.com/tanersener/ffmpeg-kit)?
I can't see why not. I never tried `ffmpeg_kit` myself but I used many other plugins that have channel calls without a single issue.

Update: There are no current plans to implement this at this time (sorry), mostly just because we're focused on higher-priority issues like improving graphics performance.

There exist a few workarounds, which have been mentioned several times in this thread: [isolate_handler](https://pub.dev/packages/isolate_handler) and [flutter_isolate](https://pub.dev/packages/flutter_isolate) are the main ones.

If you don't need to talk to Android or iOS code, but can talk to C code instead, then dart:ffi may be an interesting alternative as well; that should work fine from a normal Dart isolate.

If anyone is interested in working on implementing this feature directly in the engine, the first step would be to [create a design doc](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Design-Documents) to describe the desired approach. Alternatively, to help this issue be considered in a coming planning cycle, it would be very useful to collect the use cases for this issue in one place, e.g. a Google Doc (you can use the same template and techniques [as for design docs](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Design-Documents)) or a comment on this issue. There are many use cases listed in this issue but they are spread among 150+ comments so it is hard to see at a glance the whole picture. Details on why specific use cases aren't sufficiently handled by the workarounds above would be most useful (e.g. Sqlite is mentioned as one use case, but the best way to use Sqlite from an isolate is dart:ffi, not message channels).

In either case, please feel free to reach out on our Discord (link in the repo's contributing guidelines, start with the #hackers-new channel) to learn more about our processes if you are interested in contributing.

Finally, I would like to remind everyone that `+1`'ing an issue does nothing, but using the Thumbs-Up emoji on the first comment help surface the issue in our searches of high-demand issues, as described [on our wiki](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Issue-hygiene#thumbs-up-reactions).

I haven't thought much about this but if I were to implement this I'd probably create a static function that can setup a BinaryMessenger on a background isolate which will listen to a dart port to wake up with a integer message_id and check against its stored `Completer`s for one associated with the message_id.  On the host side the engine will store the result's binary data and send the message_id to the port so the target isolate could grab ownership of the results binary data without copying it.

I've created a design document that solves this issue.  Please give it a look if you are interested in this issue to make sure it addresses your needs: [Isolate Platform Channels](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1yAFw-6kBefuurXWTur9jdEUAckWiWJVukP1Iay8ehyU/edit#heading=h.722pnbmlqbkx).

I wrote sample code for the feature if you want to see the new feature working: https://github.com/gaaclarke/background_isolate_channels_sample

The sample code is still in flux until I can get it landed in the official samples repo.

I'm marking this closed as per our guidelines that issues are closed when fixes hit master, not stable.  The only outstanding tasks are documentation tasks that can't land until this feature hits stable.

> I haven't thought much about this but if I were to implement this I'd probably create a static function that can setup a BinaryMessenger on a background isolate which will listen to a dart port to wake up with a integer message_id and check against its stored Completers for one associated with the message_id. On the host side the engine will store the result's binary data and send the message_id to the port so the target isolate could grab ownership of the results binary data without copying it.

It all starts with an idea. Thanks for this @gaaclarke Can't wait for it to be on stable.

@venkatd Yes that's exactly what this PR would enable!  Currently movement keys (and some others) are handled in a RawKeyListener inside of RenderEditable, which is why your code above can't override their behavior.  In this PR I'm deleting the movement code in that RawKeyListener and moving it to Shortcuts.

@justinmc that's great! This is going to be a pretty high value add for our product:

- Various dropdowns with autocomplete that require arrow key traversal of the options
- Mentions of users and other entities
- Shortcut heavy chat composer

Was trying various hacks to override this stuff like saving/reverting cursor positions, but seems like it's worth waiting for this and swapping over to the dev branch when it's ready.

Subscribing...
@venkatd For autocomplete, also take a look at the new [Autocomplete](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/73753) widget which is getting keyboard shortcuts thanks to @shihaohong.
@venkatd Once I have a PR ready for it, I can cc/ you in on it so you can give it a go and provide some feedback if you like!

Thanks @justinmc !

@shihaohong sounds good! Our current use case may be slightly different because we have custom needs for our chat message composer. This includes:

- Detecting when users type types in natural language
- Switching on type (@ to mention users and # to mention tasks)

The ideal use case for us is allowing us to compose some of the hard parts like:
- Responding to text editor changes and shortcut keys to update a ValueNotifer to build our search results by hand
- Anchoring a floating widget relative to a target widget that updates in response to those changes. Can inject these same results into this floating widget. For our chat composer, this results widget needs to be anchored bottomLeft-topLeft (and matching widths) to the composer widget.

I think we do have some situations that could make use of a drop-in autocomplete replacement but several may call for custom code. Either way happy to jump in when it's ready and explain our use cases so you can decide if it's in scope.

I am having an issue where I have a `TextField` inside of an `FocusableActionDetector` which detects the Ctrl-A shortcut. However when the `TextField` is focused and I press Ctrl-A, all text is selected and the action is invoked. But the `TextField` should consume the shortcut and the action should only be invoked if the `FocusableActionDetector` or some child that does not have an action for `Ctrl-A` is focused.

Does this PR implicitly resolve that issue?

@Quijx I'm not 100% aware of the details, but it's related.

You can take a look at this file to see what previously hardcoded keyboard combinations are being converted to shortcuts:
https://github.com/justinmc/flutter/blob/text-editing-refactor/packages/flutter/lib/src/widgets/text_editing_shortcuts.dart

Anything that's converted to a shortcut can be overridden. I don't see Ctrl-A in this PR, but I'm sure it'll either be added here or in a future PR because that's the direction they want to move in. Once it's in there, you should be able to override the default OS behavior.

@Quijx +1 to what @venkatd said.  Control-A is not in this PR but I plan to add it to TextEditingShortcuts in the next PR, as soon as I get buy-in here on this approach.  Currently it is handled [here](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/7711c1ef8f478c03d12d72f790c625a194cc93b9/packages/flutter/lib/src/rendering/editable.dart#L921) in a RawKey listener, which is why it's not affected by Shortcuts.

Ready for re-review.  Thanks to everyone for taking a look.  Some stuff to look at:

  * The biggest change is that Actions, and Intents triggered by Shortcuts, are named for what they do now. See https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/75032/files#diff-a7ab0a5db9b1cb96f1d25b62073b4d8bbbe0f4dfa56578bede557c528cd0c121.
  * Are we ok with the current lack of support on web?  See https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/75032#discussion_r577779699.
  * Is there a nice way that I can document all shortcuts?  I can just put them into the docs for TextEditingShortcuts, but I worry that the docs will get out of sync with the code.  The real source of truth are the maps like `_androidShortcuts`, etc., but these are private.
  * @HansMuller A question on your onInvoke naming suggestion: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/75032/files#r577129035
Yeah I'll try to address https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/75032#discussion_r580704225 separately.

Ready for re-review.  The biggest changes this time are:

  * [TextEditingActionTarget](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/75032/files#diff-b14445f823d598a72b4823a9663ee7b3a3d4fcbe074ccc712848608e56faf798R20) decouples TextEditingAction from EditableText.
  * [TextEditingAction](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/75032/files#diff-b14445f823d598a72b4823a9663ee7b3a3d4fcbe074ccc712848608e56faf798R46) is now an abstract class and the [actions all implement it](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/75032/files#diff-a7ab0a5db9b1cb96f1d25b62073b4d8bbbe0f4dfa56578bede557c528cd0c121R113).
  * Passing actions/shortcuts to WidgetsApp [doesn't affect](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/75032/files#diff-f629b31e1aaf7209877c14f88326284538c1279b6bcf4859e3d4daebc889bef4R1636) the DefaultTextEditingActions/Shortcuts, they must be overridden with Actions/Shortcuts widgets.

Thanks again and let me know if I missed anyone's comment anywhere!
